context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this participatory development reflection done tbh — can u just give me a solid 700–800 word answer i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that assignments can feel overwhelming, but writing it for you isn't something I do — that's your job, and honestly, doing it yourself is how you actually learn.

What I *can* do is help you think through the ideas so you can write a strong reflection on your own.

Let's start simple: when you think about 'participation' in development planning, what does that word mean to you? Who is participating, and in what?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write their assignment for them, which violates my core role as a tutor. I must firmly decline, remind them of my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual content of the assignment using the Socratic method. I'll keep it brief and redirect to the prompt.

exchanges.student: idk, just give me the main points + a clean thesis i can turn in fast pls.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but that's still not something I'll do — providing a thesis or main points for you to submit crosses the line into academic dishonesty, and that's not a road I'll help you down.

I'm here to help you *think*, not to think for you.

So let's try again: in your own words, what do you think 'participation' means in the context of development projects in poorer countries? Just a gut reaction is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them. I need to firmly but briefly decline and redirect. I should not provide main points or a thesis, as that would essentially be completing the assignment for them. I'll hold my boundary clearly and try to get them engaged with the actual thinking.

exchanges.student: tbh my gut reaction is “locals being included,” but can u just make that into a polished answer for the prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start — 'locals being included' is actually a reasonable intuition to build from.

But I won't polish it for you. That's your work to do.

Here's a question to push that idea further: *included in what, exactly?* Are locals being consulted, or are they making decisions? Does that difference matter to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuine first answer ('locals being included') which is actually a decent starting point. I should acknowledge it positively and build on it using the Socratic method rather than polishing it for them. I need to push them to deepen their own thinking by asking a follow-up question that moves toward the assignment prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah it matters, but i'm short on time rn — can u at least give me a sample intro paragraph i can tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
No sample paragraphs — even ones to 'tweak' are still my writing, not yours.

But you just said something genuinely interesting: the difference between being *consulted* and actually *making decisions*. That's a real tension the prompt is asking about.

So here's my question: which one do you think counts as *true* participation, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to write for them, now framing it as just a 'sample' they can tweak. This is still doing the writing for them, which I must refuse. However, the student has shown some genuine thinking (the distinction between consultation and decision-making is meaningful). I should acknowledge their progress and redirect that insight toward building their own intro, using a targeted question.

exchanges.student: probably decision-making, but fr i just need something usable — can u list like 4 arguments i should make?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — listing arguments for you is doing the assignment, just in bullet form.

But notice what you've already figured out on your own: participation means locals being included, and real participation means decision-making power, not just being consulted. That's actually solid thinking.

The prompt asks whether participation is a *goal* or a *method* — what do you think that distinction means?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold firm. However, they've now built up two solid ideas: 'locals being included' and the consultation vs. decision-making distinction. I should acknowledge this progress and redirect their energy toward the actual prompt questions rather than listing arguments for them. I'll try to get them to engage with one of the prompt's specific questions to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: ngl idk the distinction, that's why i'm asking 😭 can u just explain that part super directly?
exchanges.tutor:
Fair enough — let me help you get there yourself.

Think about voting: is voting a *goal* (something society wants to achieve) or a *method* (a tool to reach a better outcome)? Could it be both?

Now apply that same thinking to participation in a development project.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely confused about the goal vs. method distinction, which is a legitimate conceptual hurdle. This is a case where I should help them think through it using a concrete example rather than explaining it directly. I'll use a guiding question with a simple analogy to scaffold their thinking without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: ok so it’s both lol, can u just confirm how to say that in the paper without making it sound dumb?
exchanges.tutor:
You're right that it can be both — good thinking.

But how to say it is *your* job, not mine.

Here's what I want you to think about next: if participation is *both* a goal and a method, can those two roles ever conflict with each other? Think of a real-world situation where they might.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framed as 'confirming how to say it.' I must refuse but acknowledge their correct insight. They've correctly identified that participation can be both a goal and a method. Now I should push them to articulate *why* that creates tension, which is the next step in the prompt and moves the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: maybe if participation slows a project down? i just need a yes/no tho — is that a valid point?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong example — sit with that confidence for a second.

You've now got several solid ideas: locals as decision-makers, participation as both goal and method, and the tension when participation slows things down. That's enough to start writing.

Why not try drafting an opening paragraph using those ideas? Post it here and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good point about participation slowing projects down as a potential conflict between participation as goal vs. method. Rather than giving a yes/no (which they can figure out themselves), I should push them to develop this idea further since it's substantive and moves toward the prompt. We've now had several exchanges building real ideas - I should encourage them to start integrating these into written content.

exchanges.student: i haven’t written anything yet tbh; can u at least tell me the structural factors part so i’m not guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
Still your thinking to do, not mine to hand over.

Here's a nudge: why do you think top-down development projects in the past might have *failed*? Who was left out of the planning process, and what happened as a result?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the 'structural factors' part of the prompt directly. This is still doing the intellectual work for them. However, they've built up good ideas through our conversation. Rather than explaining structural factors, I should ask a guiding question that helps them reason toward it themselves. The structural factors question in the prompt relates to why participation became central to development strategies - things like failures of top-down planning, colonialism, governance issues etc. I'll nudge them toward this with a question.

exchanges.student: maybe bc outsiders controlled it and local institutions were weak, but can u pls just tell me if that’s basically the right answer?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good instinct — colonial legacies and weak local institutions are exactly the kind of structural factors worth exploring.

Look at what you've built up across our conversation: you have ideas on what participation means, the goal vs. method tension, a concrete conflict example, and structural causes. That's your reflection, waiting to be written.

Stop second-guessing and start drafting. Write a rough first paragraph and bring it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed genuinely strong ideas across our conversation: locals as decision-makers, participation as both goal and method, tension when participation slows projects, and now outsider control and weak local institutions as structural factors. This is actually enough material for a solid reflection. Rather than validating with yes/no, I should push them to now start writing. We've hit the point where continuing to discuss without writing is unproductive - per the rules about redundancy and integrating discussion into written work.
